ojas_damle.name: Ojas Damle
ojas_damle.summary: Ojas Damle is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Ojas's.
ojas_damle.roast:
Step right up and meet Ojas Damle, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ojas is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Ojas has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ojas saga began at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC with 1 entries and a best of 65th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Ojas is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Ojas's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Ojas is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ojas has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ojas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Ojas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Ojas is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ojas wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

oliver_chung.name: Oliver Chung
oliver_chung.summary:
Representing Illinois, Oliver Chung is a tenacious competitor with 3 quarterfinal-or-better finishes, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 11 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

Notably, Oliver has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Oliver especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Oliver has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Lohith Raj Vuppala (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Anjika Pattojoshi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events)

Oliver has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Oliver brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
oliver_chung.roast:
Step right up and meet Oliver Chung, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 30 events across 11 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 30-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Oliver's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Oliver keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Oliver saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Oliver plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 30 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Oliver has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Oliver's texts. The go-to partner is Lohith Raj Vuppala (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope Nathan Park has emotionally recovered.

Oliver has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Oliver has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Oliver has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Oliver showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Oliver traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Oliver entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Oliver paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 30 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Oliver. Oliver just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Oliver has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Oliver. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Oliver: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

What makes Oliver especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Nikhil Kandalam (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #4)

Oliver has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

oliver_lien_strayer.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Oliver Lien Strayer, from Oregon, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 30 career entries in 15 tournaments, Oliver has 1 title — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 29 entries where Oliver drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 30 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Oliver saga began at 2022 NW Oregon OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 SBC NW CRC in BS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Oliver's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 9 entries — a stretch where Oliver actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Oliver is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Oliver plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Oliver is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Oliver has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Oliver's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Nikki Yanjun Lang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Oliver's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Oliver has shown up 8 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Oliver is not just losing locally — Oliver is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Strayer family travels cross-country so Oliver can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Oliver has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Oliver showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Oliver traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Oliver entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Oliver paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Oliver story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. But every season Oliver comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Oliver, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Olivia is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (GD U13, XD U15, GS U13), which means Olivia has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Olivia's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Olivia went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Olivia represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 5 career entries and a 0% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 5 entries worth of material.

The Olivia saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior with 3 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Olivia would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Olivia has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Olivia has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Olivia's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Olivia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Season after season, Olivia keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Olivia has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Olivia, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

olivia_hou.name: Olivia Hou

olivia_hou.roast:
Allow us to introduce Olivia Hou, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Olivia is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26), and so far, the only thing Olivia has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Olivia saga began at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26) with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Olivia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Olivia has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Olivia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26) in GS U11, which means Olivia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Olivia is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Olivia wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

olivia_liu.summary:
Out of New York, Olivia Liu has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 41 entries across 15 tournaments, Olivia is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

A true triple-threat, Olivia competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Olivia adapts to anyone:
  • Nila Ramesh (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Edward Chang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Olivia has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Olivia isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Olivia brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
olivia_liu.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Olivia Liu, from New York, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Olivia has 41 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Olivia saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Can we talk about Olivia and Mixed Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Olivia can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Mixed Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Olivia still cannot reach it.

If Olivia's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Olivia has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 Capital NE OLC, 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Olivia has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Olivia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 41 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Olivia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Olivia's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Austin Win: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Austin Win has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Edward Chang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Olivia has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Olivia has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Olivia has 28 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Olivia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Olivia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Olivia entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Olivia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 41 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Olivia. Olivia just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Olivia has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Olivia. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Olivia: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Olivia is 0-for-24. Zero titles in 24 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Olivia and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Olivia looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Olivia. We will be watching.

orlando_himawan.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Orlando Himawan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 24 career entries in 10 tournaments, Orlando has 1 title — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 23 entries where Orlando drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 24 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Orlando saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC, where Orlando burst onto the scene with a title in XD U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Orlando has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Orlando promised and what Orlando has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Orlando actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Orlando is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Orlando would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Orlando has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Orlando has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Orlando's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Orlando plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Orlando is 0-for-7. Zero titles in 7 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Orlando and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Orlando needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Orlando has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Stella Pan (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U17 — we hope Ethan Paw has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Orlando's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Orlando entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Orlando's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Orlando and said 'nah.'

Orlando has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Orlando is not just losing locally — Orlando is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Himawan family travels cross-country so Orlando can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Orlando has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Orlando showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U19, which means Orlando traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Orlando entered 2 events with results ranging from 3rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Orlando paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title, 24 entries, and 23 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Orlando looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Orlando. We will be watching.

oscar_ching_chun_fan.summary:
Representing California, Oscar Ching Chun Fan is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U13 level, with 6 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Oscar especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
oscar_ching_chun_fan.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Oscar Ching Chun Fan, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Oscar is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 SoCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Oscar has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Oscar saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Oscar plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 6 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Oscar has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Oscar showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13, which means Oscar traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Oscar entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Oscar paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Oscar is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Oscar wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

oscar_guanlang_he.name: Oscar Guanlang He
oscar_guanlang_he.summary:
Out of Illinois, Oscar Guanlang He is relentlessly knocking on the door, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching 3 finals across 15 entries. A player who reaches that many championship matches has all the tools — the breakthrough title is coming.

Notably, Oscar has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Oscar competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Oscar has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Oscar brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
oscar_guanlang_he.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Oscar Guanlang He, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 15 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 15-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Oscar's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Oscar keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Oscar saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Can we talk about Oscar and Boys' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Oscar can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Oscar still cannot reach it.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Oscar reaches a final. Oscar has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 Midwest OLC, where Oscar entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BD U17, XD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. And remember: Oscar has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Oscar plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Oscar has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.'

Oscar has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Oscar has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Oscar has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Oscar showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U19, which means Oscar traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Oscar entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Oscar paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Oscar keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Oscar has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Oscar knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Oscar has turned Boys' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

owen_wang.name: Owen Wang
owen_wang.summary:
Out of Oregon, Owen Wang is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

What makes Owen especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

Owen has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Yvonne Chen Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (7 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Marcus Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, across 2 seasons)

Owen has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Owen has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Owen brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
owen_wang.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Owen Wang, from Oregon, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Owen has 28 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Owen saga began at 2024 OBA NW OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Owen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 28 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Owen has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Owen's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Yvonne Chen Yang: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Yvonne Chen Yang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U11 — we hope Danny Luo has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Owen has shown up 5 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Owen is not just losing locally — Owen is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Owen can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Owen has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Owen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Owen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Owen entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Owen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Owen keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Owen has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Owen, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.
